metadata.title: Posting to YouTube
metadata.purpose: Video 4 of 'Brutalist — Claude for Video Production' on @NikBearBrown. The true record of publishing the first three videos via the YouTube Data API: why the API not Studio, what each video needs, the publisher's job, the honest gotchas (position=/manualSortRequired bug, unlisted-vs-audit privacy, quota, credential fix), the default cross-link funnel, and the machine/human split (the machine posts; you decide what goes public). Meta: built from youtube/PUBLISH-LOG.md, using the three published videos as the worked example. Basis doc: docs/posting-to-youtube.md. Rev 2 adds the CC beat: captions ship with every post — a source-text .srt built from the beat sheet, uploaded via captions.insert. Clients want their captions right.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — the master clock; every beat conforms to its measured audio
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'BrutalistTerminalOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B99: palette=claude but the outro is 'BrutalistCommentCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. You've made the videos. Now they have to go live — and how you post them matters as much as how you made them. This is posting to YouTube, done the way the machine should do it. Meta, like the rest: a video about posting the three videos before it.

production_viz.label: rendered isn't posted
production_viz.mechanic: A finished .mp4 file sitting on a disk on the left; the YouTube destination on the right; a gap between them with a red dashed line. Caption: 'a finished cut on disk is not on YouTube.' The whole video is about crossing that gap well.

beats.narration_text: Here's the gap. A finished cut sitting on your disk is not on YouTube. Crossing that gap by hand — dragging each file into Studio — means retyping the title, pasting the description, re-entering the tags, finding the playlist, and dragging the video into order. Every time.

production_viz.label: the API keeps what drag-and-drop loses
production_viz.mechanic: Two columns. LEFT 'Studio drag-and-drop': a tired list you retype every time — title, description, tags, category, find playlist, drag into order (all red, repeated). RIGHT 'the API': the same fields flowing automatically from beat_sheet.json (teal, one arrow). The automation is the point.

beats.narration_text: So the toolkit posts through the YouTube API instead. And everything travels with the upload, straight from the beat sheet: the title, a full description, the tags, the category, playlist membership, and chapter order. The automation is exactly what a drag-and-drop upload throws away.

Each video needs four things, and the pipeline already made all four. The master to upload. The full description. The beat sheet's metadata — title, tags, and a chapter number that sets the playlist order. And a captions file — the CC track, built from the beat sheet itself.

production_viz.label: find playlist → upload → order
production_viz.mechanic: A simple three-step flow: (1) find-or-create the 'Brutalist' playlist by title, (2) upload each master (resumable videos.insert), (3) add each to the playlist at its chapter position. Below, a small 'ledger' chip: 'records each ID → re-runs skip what's already up.'

beats.narration_text: The publisher does three things. It finds, or creates, the Brutalist playlist by title. It uploads each master. And it adds each video to the playlist at its chapter position — so the playlist reads in order no matter what order they uploaded. A little ledger records every ID, so a re-run skips what's already up.

production_viz.label: captions ship with the post
production_viz.mechanic: An .srt cue card (cue number, timecodes, two caption lines) with a teal arrow FROM beat_sheet.json labelled 'source text · measured timing'. Beside it, faded, an 'auto-generated' caption with a misheard phrase crossed out in red — the voice says 'h nu', auto-transcription writes 'h new', the source text shows 'E = hν'. Bottom caption: 'captions.insert — the CC track travels with the upload.' Clients want their captions right; the beat sheet already has them right.

beats.narration_text: And there's a fourth job: captions. The beat sheet already knows every word of narration and exactly when it lands — so the publisher uploads a real caption track, built from the source text on measured timing. Not an auto-transcription guess. Clients want their captions right, and the machine already has them right.

output: ch3  When Cowork Can Help Claude Code

beats.narration_text: Always dry-run first. It prints the plan: chapter one, What is Brutalist. Chapter two, Installs. Chapter three, When Cowork Can Help Claude Code. Read the order before you spend a single upload.
Then publish for real. Each upload comes back with its link, and each is added to the playlist in order. Three videos, live, in the right sequence.

production_viz.label: one real bug, one fix
production_viz.mechanic: A terminal error card: 'playlistItems.insert → 400 manualSortRequired'. Beside it, the cause: the playlist auto-sorts, but the script sent position=. The fix: a 'position=' token crossed out (red) → clean insert (teal). The ledger meant the re-run skipped the already-uploaded video. Honest record — this really happened.

beats.narration_text: And here's the honest part. The first run uploaded video one, then crashed before the playlist insert. The playlist auto-sorts, but the script was sending a position — which the API rejects for an auto-sorted playlist. Remove the position, and it's clean. The ledger meant the re-run just skipped what was already up.

production_viz.label: unlisted works; public is yours
production_viz.mechanic: Three privacy chips: PRIVATE, UNLISTED (checkmark teal — 'no audit needed'), PUBLIC (locked, red — 'needs the API compliance audit'). An arrow from UNLISTED to PUBLIC labelled 'a manual flip in Studio — you decide.' The machine posts; you choose what goes live.

beats.narration_text: Privacy is where the audit lives. All three landed unlisted, exactly as asked — so unlisted needs no audit. Public is the gated part: until a client is audited, YouTube can lock API uploads, so going public is a manual flip you make yourself in Studio. The machine posts; you decide what the world sees.

production_viz.label: ~6 uploads a day
production_viz.mechanic: A meter: 10,000 units/day total; each upload draws 1,600 → ~6 per day. Three ticks used (teal), plenty left. A small 'ledger' note: 'a longer run batches across days; the ledger tracks the boundary so it never double-posts.'

beats.narration_text: Quota is generous. Each upload costs sixteen hundred units against ten thousand a day — about six videos daily. Three was easy. A longer run just batches across days, and the ledger tracks the boundary so nothing posts twice.

production_viz.label: the funnel, by default
production_viz.mechanic: A funnel diagram: many SHORTS at the top each with an arrow to the intro VIDEO 'What is Brutalist?'; the LONGS each with an arrow to the series PLAYLIST 'Brutalist'. Caption: 'shorts point to the intro; longs to the playlist — appended to every description automatically.'

beats.narration_text: Posting also wires the series together. By default, every short's description points to the intro video, and every long's points to the series playlist. The funnel is automatic — one link, appended to every description.

production_viz.label: the three, live
production_viz.mechanic: Three stacked cards in order: 'ch1 What is Brutalist? — review cut (on purpose)', 'ch2 Installs, .env & Credentials', 'ch3 When Cowork Can Help Claude Code' — all tagged 'unlisted', all inside a 'Brutalist' playlist frame, in chapter order. The worked example this whole video is built from.

beats.narration_text: And there they are — the three this whole video is built from. What is Brutalist, posted as its review cut on purpose. Installs. And When Cowork Can Help Claude Code. All unlisted, all in the Brutalist playlist, in order.

production_viz.label: the machine posts; you decide what ships
production_viz.mechanic: The hero beat — most care. Center title in EB Garamond. LEFT, teal: the machine's mechanical publish — description, chapters, tags, playlist order, the idempotent ledger. RIGHT: a single human hand on the PUBLIC switch, still off, the videos waiting unlisted. Caption: 'it plays every part; you own what ships.' The conductor split, one more time.

beats.narration_text: So here's the split, one more time. The machine does the mechanical publish a hand-upload loses — the description, the chapters, the tags, the order, the ledger. What it does not do is decide what the world sees. The videos wait, unlisted, until you watch them and flip the switch. It plays every part; you own what ships.

output: every command, every error, every fix.
this video was built from this log.

beats.narration_text: And all of it — every command, every error, every fix — is in one honest log. Three uploaded, one bug found and fixed, everything unlisted. This video was built from that log.

That's posting to YouTube. The machine posts; you decide what goes public. Subscribe to follow the series, and find the whole toolkit at www dot brutalist dot art. You bring the taste; the machine brings the hands.
